title: Symphyotrichum carnerosanum
taxonomy: Symphyotrichum carnerosanum is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. This daisy-like plant is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. It belongs to the genus Symphyotrichum, which includes other types of coneflowers. Symphyotrichum carnerosanum is often found growing in dry areas with full sun. The classification of this plant is further broken down to the species level, reflecting its unique characteristics and distinctiveness from other coneflowers.
uses: Symphyotrichum carnerosanum, also known as the San Diego aster, has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The plant's leaves and flowers have been used to treat various ailments, including skin conditions, wounds, and respiratory issues. Its roots have been used to treat digestive problems and as a natural remedy for fever reduction. The plant's extracts have been studied for their potential use in treating allergies and other inflammatory conditions. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness and safety as a medicinal treatment.

constituents: Symphyotrichum carnerosanum, a type of plant, contains various active constituents, including sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids. intro: Symphyotrichum carnerosanum, also known as Camarillo Michaelmas daisy, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Asteraceae family. This plant has been used in traditional medicine to treat various health conditions, including respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma, due to its expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Symphyotrichum carnerosanum include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Herbal preparations made from this plant include teas, tinctures, and infusions, often used to relieve coughs, colds, and fever, as well as to promote relaxation and reduce inflammation.
